ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a specific code for wholemeal bread with kaya and peanut butter as a composite dish; A000E (Bread and similar products) used as the closest defensible broader category anchor.

serving.description: 2 slices of wholemeal bread with kaya and peanut butter
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving is approximately 100 g, equivalent to two slices of wholemeal bread each spread with kaya and peanut butter, commonly consumed as a breakfast or snack in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Wholemeal bread with kaya and peanut butter — HPB Singapore Food Composition Database (Foodo)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values sourced directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database record F05020142 for wholemeal bread with kaya and peanut butter. All per-100g values are taken verbatim from the HPB record. Saturated fat estimated at 3.2 g/100g based on HPB component breakdown for kaya and peanut butter contributions; monounsaturated, polyunsaturated, and trans fat data not reported in the HPB record and set to -1.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of Wholemeal / Whole Wheat Bread — University of Sydney GI Database
gi_source.source_match_notes: The GI of 69 is derived from the University of Sydney GI Database entry for wholemeal/whole wheat bread (GI ~69, medium category). The composite dish (wholemeal bread with kaya and peanut butter) does not have a direct GI measurement; the GI is estimated using the wholemeal bread anchor as the dominant carbohydrate contributor. Kaya and peanut butter contribute fat and protein which may modestly lower the effective GI, but the wholemeal bread GI of 69 is retained as a conservative estimate. Glycemic load of 26.7 per 100 g serving calculated as (GI × carbohydrate_g) / 100 = (69 × 38.75) / 100 ≈ 26.7.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium 310 mg/100 g is in the borderline-elevated range; partially offset by potassium 110 mg/100 g and dietary fibre 4.83 g/100 g.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit portion size to one serving and choose low-sodium peanut butter to keep total sodium intake within the DASH diet target of under 2,300 mg/day; the dietary fibre content supports arterial health but does not fully compensate for the sodium load.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Medium GI of 69 and carbohydrate 38.75 g/100 g; dietary fibre 4.83 g/100 g and protein 12.72 g/100 g moderate the glycaemic response.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should limit to one serving (approximately 100 g) and monitor total carbohydrate intake, as the medium GI and moderate carbohydrate load can raise postprandial blood glucose; the high fibre and protein content help slow gastric emptying, but kaya's added sugar should be minimised by using a thin spread.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat estimated at 3.2 g/100 g (below 5 g threshold) and sodium 310 mg/100 g; wholemeal bread provides 4.83 g/100 g dietary fibre which supports LDL reduction.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For individuals with cardiovascular disease, the dietary fibre from wholemeal bread supports LDL cholesterol reduction, but the sodium content of 310 mg/100 g warrants portion control; choose natural peanut butter without hydrogenated oils and limit kaya spread to reduce saturated fat and added sugar intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Dietary fibre 4.83 g/100 g supports anti-inflammatory pathways; saturated fat estimated at 3.2 g/100 g is below the 5 g threshold.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Wholemeal bread with peanut butter is generally suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to its high fibre and unsaturated fat profile; use natural peanut butter without hydrogenated oils and limit kaya to reduce saturated fat from coconut milk.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Wholemeal bread with kaya and peanut butter is a popular Singaporean breakfast and snack dish consisting of sliced wholemeal (whole wheat) bread spread with kaya — a sweet coconut and egg jam flavoured with pandan — and peanut butter. The dish is a staple of traditional Singaporean kopitiam (coffee shop) culture and is commonly served alongside kopi (local coffee) or teh (tea). The wholemeal bread provides a dense, slightly nutty texture and a mildly earthy flavour, while kaya contributes a sweet, fragrant, and creamy character and peanut butter adds richness, saltiness, and a roasted nut flavour. Nutritionally, the dish delivers 308 kcal per 100 g with 12.72 g protein, 11.67 g fat, and 38.75 g carbohydrate. Dietary fibre is notably high at 4.83 g/100 g from the wholemeal flour, which moderates the glycaemic response despite the medium GI of 69. Sodium is 310 mg/100 g, which is borderline elevated and warrants portion awareness for individuals managing hypertension. The dish is suitable for most adults as a moderate-energy breakfast but should be consumed in controlled portions by individuals with Type 2 Diabetes or cardiovascular disease due to its medium glycaemic index, moderate sodium, and added sugar from kaya. Peanut butter contributes unsaturated fats and vitamin E, while kaya adds coconut-derived saturated fat and sugar. The combination is vegetarian and halal-friendly, making it widely accessible across Singapore's multicultural population.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (record F05020142) with confidence 0.95 as reported in the input. The HPB is the primary approved source for Singaporean dishes. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database using the wholemeal bread anchor, which is the dominant carbohydrate contributor. Saturated fat is estimated from component analysis of kaya and peanut butter contributions and documented accordingly.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, and trans_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database record F05020142 as of 2026. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this composite dish is not reported in the HPB record. vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, and selenium_mcg are set to -1: micronutrient data beyond the HPB-reported fields is not available in the HPB Foodo record for this composite dish as of 2026. Saturated fat of 3.2 g/100g is estimated from the known composition of kaya (coconut milk base) and peanut butter contributions and is not directly reported in the HPB record; this value should be verified against a future HPB update. GI of 69 is an analogous estimate based on the University of Sydney GI Database entry for wholemeal/whole wheat bread as the dominant carbohydrate source; no direct GI measurement exists for this specific composite dish. Glycemic load of 26.7 calculated as (69 × 38.75) / 100.
